text: Hello, I'm sharing a sigmoid resection for perforated diverticulitis and a collocutaneous fistula to the left lower abdominal wall. This is a patient had a prior open hysterectomy. That's what the adhesions of the omentum to the lower midline are. It was quite a large abscess into the abdominal wall that actually covered the lower midline.

The surgeon carefully dissects the omental adhesions from the colon, which is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall. The colon becomes visible as the omentum is separated, allowing for restoration of normal anatomy in preparation for the sigmoid resection.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to amputate the remaining omentum from the area of perforation, with the IR drain visible and buried in the abdominal wall due to the collocutaneous fistula. The omentum is reflected into the upper abdomen, and the small bowel is moved out of the pelvis to begin the medial to lateral dissection for the sigmoid resection.

The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery and proceeds to divide it. The lateral disease is assessed, prompting further medial dissection. Indocyanine green (ICG) and stents are utilized to identify the ureter, ensuring safe dissection.

The surgeon begins dissecting the proximal mesentery and proceeds with the lateral dissection for the fistula takedown. The dissection starts inferiorly near the pelvis, staying just beneath the supportive bladder ligament. The colon is visibly adhered to the left side wall, with the surgeon working cautiously near the iliac vessels.

The surgeon identifies the perforation site where the diverticulum has herniated into the abdominal wall, forming a fistula. The dissection continues to free the colon from the left pelvic sidewall, ensuring no residual abscess remains.

The surgeon identifies the left common iliac artery and notes a thermal injury created during the dissection. The fat covering the vessels was inadvertently pulled down with the colon, leading to the injury. The surgeon elects to over-sew the thermal injury using a 4-0 Prolene suture, and adjusts the dissection to return to the proper anatomical plane.

The surgeon performs lateral mobilization to increase the mobility of the proximal colon, working to release adhesions from the left pelvic side wall. The use of ICG fluorescence aids in identifying the ureter, which is being pulled along with the colon during this maneuver.

The surgeon completes the attachments, freeing the colon entirely. The rectal mobilization is performed below the peritoneal reflection for additional mobility. The splenic flexure is mobilized using a lateral approach, with the patient positioned in steep Trendelenburg and left side up, utilizing the same ports without redocking.

The surgeon continues the dissection, separating the transverse mesocolon from the pancreas and removing the omentum from the transverse colon. The stomach is visible in the surgical field. The proximal mesentery is further dissected to reduce tension for the upcoming anastomosis.

The surgeon demonstrates rectal mobilization and proceeds to take the rectal mesentery. The decision is made to perform a transvaginal extraction of the specimen, keeping it intact, rather than a transrectal extraction. The surgeon prepares to staple the proximal section.

The surgeon staples the distal end of the colon and temporarily moves the specimen aside. An EEA sizer is inserted through the vagina, held by a nurse, to facilitate specimen extraction. A 15mm endocatch bag is used to encase the specimen, allowing it to be pulled out through the vaginotomy by closing the bag around the specimen and pulling the bag's opening outside, enabling the colon to slip out through the bag.

The surgeon pulls the specimen out through the vaginotomy by opening the end of the endocatch bag and gently pulling on the staple line, allowing it to slide out easily. The vaginotomy is then closed using PDS sutures, with the surgeon taking careful bites of the mucosa and vaginal wall.

This whole process takes a lot less time than it would to make a fan and seal extraction incision opening and closing. Again, I wanted to avoid a fan and seal in this case because the adherence where the fistula was was in that vicinity and I didn't want to have to make an incision there and I didn't want to have to make an incision anywhere else in the abdominal wall. Often is the case these patients are on the larger side. So making that incision and trying to keep it small can be quite challenging. And so this process of making this incision is significantly easier and less time consuming. That's why these cases can actually be faster than conventional. If you were going to be doing a stapled anastomosis, you would have introduced your anvil through that vaginotomy. You can see I'm running that PDS back to the original corner. kind of a two layer closure. And then we'll tie that to the original post.

The surgeon prepares to create the anastomosis, positioning it just beneath the site of the vaginal closure. The proximity of the two suture lines is visible, and the surgeon will demonstrate techniques to mitigate potential issues arising from their closeness.

The surgeon performs a standard sewn two-layer anastomosis by joining the two posterior staple lines using PDS sutures. A colotomy and proctotomy are made, followed by the application of ICG to ensure both inner lips illuminate, with the rectal side showing a slight delay before lighting up. The inner layer is sewn using 3-0 V-Loc sutures.

The surgeon completes the second anterior outer layer of the anastomosis using 2-0 PDS sutures, visible through the laparoscopic view provided by the camera positioned in port three. The intracorporeal suturing is performed without barbs, followed by a leak test to ensure the integrity of the anastomosis. The thermal burn appears visually acceptable.

The surgeon uses a 19mm proctoscope to check for leaks at the anastomosis site, which traverses easily. An omental flap is sutured to the perineal pouch of Douglas below the peritoneal reflection, separating the vaginal suture line from the anastomosis. Additional sutures are placed to cover the iliac area with omentum, addressing a visible thermal burn.
Did not leave a drain in this case. The patient does very well with an early post-op discharge. I hope you enjoyed.
